answer:
1. Definition & Core Concept

Jest Unit Test performance bottlenecks appear as test suites grow: slow startup (loading heavy dependencies per test file), excessive mocking overhead, file system I/O from snapshot tests, memory leaks between tests (no proper cleanup), and serial execution when tests could run in parallel. At scale, a 500-test suite that takes 5 minutes blocks CI pipelines and destroys developer feedback loops.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System's Fastify backend, the Jest test suite grew to 200 tests across 30 files. Run time ballooned from 30s to 4 minutes. Manoj profiled with jest --verbose --logHeapUsage and found: (1) Each test file imported PrismaClient, adding 800ms startup per file. Fix: mock Prisma at the module level. (2) Snapshot tests for API responses generated 50 snapshot files, with filesystem writes adding 500ms. Fix: inline assertions instead of snapshots for small responses. (3) No --maxWorkers tuning — Jest spawned 8 workers on a 2-core CI machine, causing thrashing. Fix: --maxWorkers=2 on CI.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// jest.config.ts — optimized for CI performance
export default {
  preset: 'ts-jest',
  testEnvironment: 'node',
  maxWorkers: process.env.CI ? 2 : '50%', // Match CI core count
  clearMocks: true,             // Auto-clear mock state between tests
  restoreMocks: true,           // Auto-restore original implementations
  testTimeout: 10000,           // Fail fast on stuck tests
  coverageProvider: 'v8',       // Faster than babel coverage
  moduleNameMapper: {
    // Mock heavy modules at config level
    '@prisma/client': '/src/__mocks__/prisma.ts',
  },
};

// src/__mocks__/prisma.ts — lightweight mock
import { mockDeep } from 'jest-mock-extended';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
export const prisma = mockDeep();
export default prisma;

// Test file: no PrismaClient startup cost
import { prisma } from '../__mocks__/prisma';
import { LeaveService } from '../services/leave.service';

describe('LeaveService', () => {
  const service = new LeaveService(prisma);

  afterEach(() => {
    jest.restoreAllMocks(); // Prevent memory leaks
  });

  it('should reject leave on weekends', async () => {
    const saturday = new Date('2025-03-08'); // Saturday
    await expect(
      service.submit({ startDate: saturday, days: 1, userId: 'u1' })
    ).rejects.toThrow('No weekends');
    // Inline assertion — no snapshot file
  });

  it('should deduct balance on approval', async () => {
    prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique.mockResolvedValue({ remaining: 10 });
    prisma.leave.create.mockResolvedValue({ id: 'l1', status: 'pending' });

    const result = await service.submit({ startDate: new Date('2025-03-10'), days: 2, userId: 'u1' });
    expect(result.status).toBe('pending');
    expect(prisma.leaveBalance.update).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
      expect.objectContaining({ data: { remaining: { decrement: 2 } } })
    );
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The HR system's CI pipeline took 8 minutes total — 4 of those were Jest tests. Manoj ran jest --verbose --logHeapUsage and spotted that heap usage grew from 100MB to 800MB across the suite (memory leak). The culprit: a Fastify server instance created in beforeAll but never closed in afterAll — each test file leaked a server. Adding afterAll(() => app.close()) and jest.restoreAllMocks() in afterEach stabilized heap at 200MB. Combined with module-level Prisma mocking and CI-tuned maxWorkers, the suite dropped from 4 minutes to 45 seconds.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Importing real PrismaClient in tests800ms startup per test fileModule-level mock via moduleNameMapper
Too many snapshot testsDisk I/O + brittle updatesInline assertions for small responses; snapshots only for complex UI
maxWorkers not tuned for CIWorker thrashing on limited coresSet maxWorkers to CI core count
No afterEach cleanupMemory leaks, flaky tests from leaked stateclearMocks + restoreMocks in jest.config
Serial test executionTests run one-at-a-time despite being independentEnsure tests have no shared state; let Jest parallelize

7. Validation Strategy

  • CI time budget: Jest suite must complete in < 60s; alert if exceeded.
  • Heap monitoring: --logHeapUsage in CI; alert if peak > 500MB (leak indicator).
  • Leak detection: Run jest --detectLeaks weekly; fail if any test file leaks.
  • Worker tuning: Benchmark maxWorkers=1,2,4 on CI; pick the fastest configuration.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Jest Unit Tests deep dive for interviewer grade covers: the testing pyramid (unit > integration > E2E by count), what constitutes a good unit test (fast, isolated, deterministic, self-validating), mocking vs stubbing vs spying trade-offs, test-driven development workflow, and failure modes (false positives from over-mocking, false negatives from under-testing, brittle tests coupled to implementation). The key trade-off: highly mocked tests run fast but may not catch real integration bugs; less-mocked tests are more realistic but slower and harder to maintain.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj's unit testing strategy: LeaveValidator — pure function tests with zero mocks (fastest, most reliable). LeaveService — Prisma mocked, tests verify business logic (no weekend leaves, balance check, overlap detection). ApprovalService — strategy pattern tested by injecting mock strategies. Trade-off: the LeaveService tests mocked Prisma so heavily that a real bug (Prisma query returning null instead of throwing) wasn't caught by unit tests — only by integration tests. Failure mode: a refactor renamed findUnique to findFirst, but the mock still used findUnique — test passed (mock didn't care about method name), but production broke. Rollback plan: pair unit tests with integration tests that use a real test database.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Good unit test: tests behavior, not implementation
describe('LeaveValidator', () => {
  // Pure function — no mocks needed
  it('rejects leave starting on Saturday', () => {
    const result = LeaveValidator.validate({
      startDate: new Date('2025-03-08'), // Saturday
      endDate: new Date('2025-03-10'),
      days: 1,
    });
    expect(result.ok).toBe(false);
    expect(result.errors).toContain('Cannot start leave on weekend');
  });

  it('rejects backdated leave', () => {
    const yesterday = new Date(Date.now() - 86400000);
    const result = LeaveValidator.validate({
      startDate: yesterday,
      endDate: yesterday,
      days: 1,
    });
    expect(result.ok).toBe(false);
    expect(result.errors).toContain('Cannot backdate leave');
  });
});

// Service test: mock dependency, test business logic
describe('LeaveService.submit', () => {
  const mockRepo = {
    findBalance: jest.fn(),
    createLeave: jest.fn(),
    deductBalance: jest.fn(),
  };
  const service = new LeaveService(mockRepo); // DI

  afterEach(() => jest.clearAllMocks());

  it('rejects when balance insufficient', async () => {
    mockRepo.findBalance.mockResolvedValue({ remaining: 1 });

    await expect(
      service.submit({ userId: 'u1', days: 5, startDate: new Date('2025-03-10') })
    ).rejects.toThrow('Insufficient balance');

    // Behavior check: leave NOT created
    expect(mockRepo.createLeave).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
  });

  it('creates leave and deducts balance when valid', async () => {
    mockRepo.findBalance.mockResolvedValue({ remaining: 10 });
    mockRepo.createLeave.mockResolvedValue({ id: 'l1' });

    const result = await service.submit({
      userId: 'u1', days: 3, startDate: new Date('2025-03-10'),
    });

    expect(result.id).toBe('l1');
    // Behavior: balance deducted by correct amount
    expect(mockRepo.deductBalance).toHaveBeenCalledWith('u1', 3);
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

A production bug slipped past 200 unit tests: when a user submitted a leave for exactly their remaining balance (e.g., 5 days remaining, requesting 5 days), the balance became 0 but the system showed an error "Insufficient balance" on the next page load. The unit test mocked findBalance to return { remaining: 10 } and tested against days: 5 — it passed because 10 >= 5. The edge case of remaining === days was never tested. Manoj added boundary tests: remaining: 5, days: 5 (should succeed) and remaining: 4, days: 5 (should fail). The bug was a > instead of >= in the balance check. Lesson: always test boundary conditions, not just happy paths.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Over-mocking (always return success)False confidence; real errors uncaughtMock returns realistic data including edge cases
Testing implementation detailsTests break on refactor without behavior changeTest inputs/outputs, not which method was called
No boundary testingEdge cases cause production bugsTest at boundaries: 0, 1, max, max+1, exact match
Shared state between testsTest order dependency → flaky testsclearMocks + restoreMocks + no global state
Unit tests only (no integration)Mocks don't catch real integration bugsTesting pyramid: units + integration + E2E

7. Validation Strategy

  • Coverage quality: Require branch coverage > 80% (not just line coverage — catches missing else/edge paths).
  • Mutation testing: Run Stryker monthly; score > 70% means tests catch real bugs, not just code paths.
  • Boundary test audit: Review all numeric comparisons; ensure tests exist for =, <, > boundary values.
  • False positive check: Deliberately break a business rule; verify at least one test fails.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Mocking Strategy deep dive covers the spectrum from no mocks (real dependencies) to full mocks (every dependency replaced), including: jest.mock (module-level), jest.spyOn (method-level), manual mocks (__mocks__ directory), dependency injection for testability, and mock libraries like jest-mock-extended for typed mocks. Trade-offs: more mocking = faster tests but less confidence in integration; less mocking = slower tests but catches real bugs. Failure modes: mocks that don't match the real API (interface drift), over-asserting on mock calls (brittle tests), and forgetting to restore mocks (test pollution).

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj used a layered mocking strategy: No mocks for LeaveValidator (pure functions). Repository mocks for LeaveService (inject mock repo interface, not mock Prisma directly). Module mock for Socket.io (jest.mock('socket.io') — don't send real notifications in tests). Spy on console.error to verify error logging. In ContactsHub, Mongoose models were mocked using jest-mock-extended's mockDeep to preserve TypeScript types. Failure mode: the mock for prisma.leave.findMany returned an array, but in production Prisma returned a Prisma-specific array with extra methods — a test that called .map() worked on both, but one that checked instanceof failed.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Layered mocking strategy for HR Leave System

// 1. NO MOCK — Pure function testing
describe('LeaveValidator', () => {
  it('detects weekend start date', () => {
    expect(LeaveValidator.isWeekend(new Date('2025-03-08'))).toBe(true);
  });
});

// 2. INTERFACE MOCK — DI-based service testing
interface LeaveRepository {
  findBalance(userId: string): Promise<{ remaining: number }>;
  createLeave(data: any): Promise<{ id: string }>;
}

function createMockRepo(): jest.Mocked {
  return {
    findBalance: jest.fn(),
    createLeave: jest.fn(),
  };
}

describe('LeaveService', () => {
  const repo = createMockRepo();
  const service = new LeaveService(repo);

  it('calls repo with correct data', async () => {
    repo.findBalance.mockResolvedValue({ remaining: 10 });
    repo.createLeave.mockResolvedValue({ id: 'l1' });

    await service.submit({ userId: 'u1', days: 3 });

    expect(repo.createLeave).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
      expect.objectContaining({ userId: 'u1', days: 3 })
    );
  });
});

// 3. MODULE MOCK — External service
jest.mock('socket.io', () => ({
  Server: jest.fn().mockImplementation(() => ({
    to: jest.fn().mockReturnThis(),
    emit: jest.fn(),
  })),
}));

// 4. SPY — Observe without replacing
describe('ErrorHandler', () => {
  it('logs errors to console', () => {
    const spy = jest.spyOn(console, 'error').mockImplementation();
    handleError(new Error('test'));
    expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.stringContaining('test'));
    spy.mockRestore();
  });
});

// 5. TYPED MOCK with jest-mock-extended
import { mockDeep } from 'jest-mock-extended';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
const prisma = mockDeep();
// TypeScript catches if you mock a method that doesn't exist!

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

A bug in the HR system's approval notification went undetected for a week: the Socket.io emit was called with the wrong event name ('leave-update' instead of 'leave-updated'). The unit test mocked Socket.io and asserted emit was called — but didn't check the event name. The test passed, but the Angular frontend listened for 'leave-updated' and never received the notification. Manoj fixed the test to assert the exact event name: expect(mockIo.emit).toHaveBeenCalledWith('leave-updated', expect.any(Object)). He also added a contract test that verified the event name matched between backend emission and frontend listener. Lesson: mock assertions must verify the meaningful parameters, not just "was it called?".

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Mock doesn't match real APITests pass, production breaks (interface drift)Use typed mocks (jest-mock-extended); contract tests
Asserting mock was called (no args check)Wrong arguments go undetectedAssert with expect.objectContaining for key params
Mocking too deep (Prisma internals)Tests coupled to ORM implementationMock at repository interface level, not ORM level
Not restoring mocks between testsTest pollution → flaky failuresrestoreMocks: true in jest.config
No integration tests to back up mocksMocks give false confidencePair unit mocks with real-DB integration tests

7. Validation Strategy

  • Interface drift check: TypeScript compilation of mock types catches API mismatches at build time.
  • Mock audit: Review mock assertions quarterly; ensure key parameters are verified (not just call count).
  • Integration backup: Every mocked service has at least 1 integration test with real dependency.
  • Contract test: Frontend event names match backend emission names (verified in CI).

1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling Mocking Strategy for 1 M+ users means designing a test infrastructure where mocks scale with the codebase: shared mock factories, mock registries, test containers for realistic mocks, and ensuring mocks stay synchronized with production APIs as the system grows. At scale with 50+ microservices, each service needs mocks for its dependencies — manually maintaining these becomes unsustainable.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

Scaling the HR Leave Management System's test suite, Manoj would implement: (1) Mock Factory: a createMockLeave() function that generates realistic Leave objects with all required fields, reducing boilerplate across 200 tests. (2) Test Container: a Docker PostgreSQL container for integration tests, replacing Prisma mocks for critical paths (approval chain, balance deduction). (3) Mock Registry: a shared __mocks__ directory with typed mocks for all external services (Socket.io, email, Claude AI), versioned alongside the code. (4) Contract-driven mocks: Pact files generated from the Angular frontend define the expected API shape, and backend mocks auto-validate against these contracts.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Mock factory pattern for HR Leave System
// src/test/factories.ts

let counter = 0;

export function createMockLeave(overrides: Partial = {}): Leave {
  counter++;
  return {
    id: `leave-${counter}`,
    userId: 'user-1',
    startDate: new Date('2025-03-10'),
    endDate: new Date('2025-03-12'),
    days: 3,
    status: 'pending',
    version: 1,
    leaveType: 'annual',
    createdAt: new Date(),
    ...overrides,
  };
}

export function createMockUser(overrides: Partial = {}): User {
  counter++;
  return {
    id: `user-${counter}`,
    name: `Test User ${counter}`,
    email: `user${counter}@test.com`,
    role: 'employee',
    departmentId: 'dept-1',
    ...overrides,
  };
}

// Usage in tests — clean, consistent, DRY
import { createMockLeave, createMockUser } from '../test/factories';

describe('ApprovalService', () => {
  it('approves pending leave at level 1', async () => {
    const leave = createMockLeave({ status: 'pending' });
    const manager = createMockUser({ role: 'team_lead' });
    mockRepo.findLeave.mockResolvedValue(leave);

    const result = await service.approve(leave.id, manager.id, 1);
    expect(result.status).toBe('level1_approved');
  });

  it('rejects approval of already-approved leave', async () => {
    const leave = createMockLeave({ status: 'approved' });
    mockRepo.findLeave.mockResolvedValue(leave);

    await expect(service.approve(leave.id, 'mgr1', 1))
      .rejects.toThrow('Already processed');
  });
});

// Test container setup for integration tests
import { PostgreSqlContainer } from '@testcontainers/postgresql';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

let container;
let prisma: PrismaClient;

beforeAll(async () => {
  container = await new PostgreSqlContainer().start();
  process.env.DATABASE_URL = container.getConnectionUri();
  prisma = new PrismaClient();
  await prisma.$executeRaw`CREATE TABLE ...`; // Run migrations
}, 30000);

afterAll(async () => {
  await prisma.$disconnect();
  await container.stop();
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

As the HR system grew from 50 to 200 tests, mock data duplication became a maintenance nightmare — 30 tests each hardcoded a Leave object with slightly different fields. When Manoj added the leaveType column, he had to update 30 test files. After introducing the factory pattern (createMockLeave()), adding leaveType required updating only the factory function — all 200 tests automatically got the new field with a sensible default. This also caught tests that incorrectly assumed a Leave had no leaveType — the factory's default exposed those assumptions immediately.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Duplicated mock data across testsSchema change requires updating 30+ filesCentralized mock factories with overrides
Mocks not typedMock returns wrong shape, test passes, prod crashesUse jest-mock-extended or typed factory functions
No integration tests backing mocksMocks diverge from real behavior over timeTest containers for critical paths in CI
Manual mock maintenance50+ services × N mocks = unmaintainableContract-driven mock generation (Pact)
Test container startup time30s per test run for Docker containerReuse container across test files; parallel test suites

7. Validation Strategy

  • Factory coverage: Every model type has a factory function; no raw object literals in tests.
  • Schema sync: Factory function TypeScript types match Prisma/Mongoose schema; compile error if out of sync.
  • Container test: At least 1 integration test per critical service using real DB container.
  • Contract sync: Pact verification runs in CI; fail if backend mock doesn't match frontend contract.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Integration Testing deep dive covers tests that verify the interaction between two or more real components — database queries with real PostgreSQL, API routes with real Fastify server, or service-to-service communication. Trade-offs: integration tests catch real bugs that unit tests miss (query errors, serialization issues, middleware behavior) but are slower (100-500ms per test vs 1-10ms for units), harder to debug (more moving parts), and require infrastructure (test database). Failure modes: test pollution from shared database state, flaky tests from timing/ordering, and slow CI from too many integration tests.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, integration tests verified the full request lifecycle: HTTP request → Fastify route → LeaveService → Prisma → PostgreSQL (test container) → HTTP response. These caught bugs that unit tests missed: (1) A Prisma query that returned null instead of throwing when a leave didn't exist — the unit test's mock returned the expected error, but Prisma actually returned null. (2) The JWT middleware rejected tokens with an unexpected iat format — only caught when testing with a real Fastify instance. (3) The 3-level approval chain's transaction isolation — only reproducible with a real PostgreSQL instance. In ContactsHub, Playwright E2E tests covered the full stack but were slow (30s per test); integration tests covered API + MongoDB in 200ms per test.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Integration test setup with Fastify + Prisma + test container
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { PostgreSqlContainer } from '@testcontainers/postgresql';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
import { buildApp } from '../app';

let container: any;
let prisma: PrismaClient;
let app: any;

beforeAll(async () => {
  // Start real PostgreSQL in Docker
  container = await new PostgreSqlContainer().start();
  process.env.DATABASE_URL = container.getConnectionUri();

  // Run migrations
  prisma = new PrismaClient();
  await prisma.$executeRawUnsafe(`CREATE TABLE ...`); // or prisma migrate

  // Build real Fastify app
  app = await buildApp({ prisma });
}, 30000);

afterAll(async () => {
  await app.close();
  await prisma.$disconnect();
  await container.stop();
});

// Clean database between tests (no pollution)
afterEach(async () => {
  await prisma.approval.deleteMany();
  await prisma.leave.deleteMany();
  await prisma.leaveBalance.deleteMany();
  await prisma.user.deleteMany();
});

describe('POST /api/leaves', () => {
  it('creates leave and deducts balance (full integration)', async () => {
    // Seed real data
    const user = await prisma.user.create({
      data: { name: 'Manoj', email: 'manoj@test.com', role: 'employee' },
    });
    await prisma.leaveBalance.create({
      data: { userId: user.id, total: 24, remaining: 24 },
    });

    // Real HTTP request
    const response = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST',
      url: '/api/leaves',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${generateTestJWT(user)}` },
      payload: {
        startDate: '2025-03-10',
        endDate: '2025-03-12',
        days: 3,
        leaveType: 'annual',
      },
    });

    expect(response.statusCode).toBe(201);
    const body = JSON.parse(response.body);
    expect(body.status).toBe('pending');

    // Verify database state
    const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({
      where: { userId: user.id },
    });
    expect(balance.remaining).toBe(21); // 24 - 3
  });

  it('returns 400 for weekend start date', async () => {
    const user = await prisma.user.create({ data: { name: 'Test', email: 't@t.com' } });
    const response = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST',
      url: '/api/leaves',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${generateTestJWT(user)}` },
      payload: { startDate: '2025-03-08', endDate: '2025-03-08', days: 1 },
    });
    expect(response.statusCode).toBe(400);
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The most valuable integration test caught a bug that 200 unit tests missed: the leave balance deduction used prisma.leaveBalance.update({ data: { remaining: remaining - days } }) — a read-then-write race condition. Two concurrent leave submissions could both read remaining: 24 and both write remaining: 21, losing 3 days. The unit test mocked Prisma and didn't reproduce concurrency. The integration test ran two concurrent app.inject() calls against the real PostgreSQL container and detected the race — the final balance was 21 instead of 18. Manoj fixed it with { remaining: { decrement: days } } (atomic operation) and the integration test verified the fix under concurrency.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Shared DB state between testsTest order dependency → flaky failuresClean DB in afterEach (deleteMany on all tables)
Too many integration testsCI takes 10+ minutesKeep integration tests focused (30 critical paths); rest as unit tests
No test container (mocked DB)Misses real DB behavior (null vs throw, constraints)Use @testcontainers for real PostgreSQL/MongoDB
Not testing concurrent scenariosRace conditions only found in productionIntegration tests with Promise.all for concurrent requests
Slow test container startup30s overhead per test runReuse container across test files; parallel suites

7. Validation Strategy

  • Critical path coverage: Every business-critical flow (submit, approve, reject, balance check) has an integration test.
  • Concurrency test: At least 1 test per write endpoint runs concurrent requests; verifies no race conditions.
  • CI time budget: Integration suite < 2 minutes; alert if exceeded.
  • Test isolation: Run tests in random order (jest --randomize); all must pass regardless of order.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling Integration Testing for 1 M+ users means designing a test infrastructure that validates the system works correctly under realistic conditions: test environments that mirror production topology, data volumes representative of scale, parallel test execution across services, and automated test data management. At scale, integration tests must validate not just correctness but also performance characteristics (query times, connection pool behavior, cache hit rates).

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

Scaling the HR Leave Management System's integration tests, Manoj would implement: (1) Seeded test database with 100k leaves (production-like volume) to verify query plans use indexes. (2) Parallel test suites: each test file gets its own schema within the shared PostgreSQL container (no cross-contamination). (3) Performance assertions: integration tests assert that API response time < 200ms (catches missing indexes or N+1 regressions). (4) Contract tests: the Angular frontend's expected API shape is validated against the real Fastify response. For ContactsHub, integration tests verified the bulk CSV upload (500 contacts) completed within 5 seconds against a real MongoDB container.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Scale-aware integration test with performance assertions
describe('GET /api/leaves (scale)', () => {
  beforeAll(async () => {
    // Seed 100k leaves for realistic query plan testing
    const users = Array.from({ length: 100 }, (_, i) =>
      prisma.user.create({ data: { name: `User ${i}`, email: `u${i}@t.com` } })
    );
    const createdUsers = await Promise.all(users);

    // Bulk insert 100k leaves
    const leaves = createdUsers.flatMap(user =>
      Array.from({ length: 1000 }, () => ({
        userId: user.id,
        startDate: randomDate(),
        endDate: randomDate(),
        days: Math.ceil(Math.random() * 5),
        status: ['pending', 'approved', 'rejected'][Math.floor(Math.random() * 3)],
      }))
    );
    await prisma.leave.createMany({ data: leaves });
  }, 60000);

  it('responds within 200ms for paginated query on 100k rows', async () => {
    const start = performance.now();
    const response = await app.inject({
      method: 'GET',
      url: '/api/leaves?status=pending&limit=50',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${testJWT}` },
    });
    const duration = performance.now() - start;

    expect(response.statusCode).toBe(200);
    expect(duration).toBeLessThan(200); // Performance assertion
    expect(JSON.parse(response.body).length).toBeLessThanOrEqual(50);
  });

  it('uses index scan on 100k rows (not seq scan)', async () => {
    const plan = await prisma.$queryRaw`
      EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON)
      SELECT * FROM "Leave"
      WHERE status = 'pending' AND "userId" = 'user-1'
      LIMIT 50
    `;
    const nodeType = plan[0]['QUERY PLAN'][0]['Plan']['Node Type'];
    expect(nodeType).not.toBe('Seq Scan');
  });

  it('handles 50 concurrent requests without pool exhaustion', async () => {
    const requests = Array.from({ length: 50 }, () =>
      app.inject({
        method: 'GET',
        url: '/api/leaves?limit=10',
        headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${testJWT}` },
      })
    );
    const responses = await Promise.all(requests);
    const failures = responses.filter(r => r.statusCode !== 200);
    expect(failures.length).toBe(0); // All succeed
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

A performance regression slipped into production: a new Prisma include for approvals caused the leave list endpoint to go from 30ms to 300ms. Unit tests didn't catch it (mocked Prisma). Manoj added a performance assertion to the integration test: expect(duration).toBeLessThan(200). On the next CI run, this test failed — the developer saw "Expected < 200, received 300" and investigated. The fix was adding a select clause to limit the included fields. After this incident, every list endpoint's integration test included a performance assertion, turning CI into a performance regression detector.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Testing with empty databaseQuery plans differ at 100 vs 100k rowsSeed realistic data volumes in test container
No performance assertionsRegressions only caught in productionAssert response time < threshold in integration tests
Serial test execution30 integration tests × 500ms = 15sParallel schemas + Jest's parallel workers
Test data not representativeTests pass on uniform data, fail on skewed production dataSeed with realistic distributions (80% pending, 15% approved, 5% rejected)
No concurrent request testingConnection pool issues only in productionPromise.all with 50+ concurrent requests in test

7. Validation Strategy

  • Performance budget: Every list endpoint integration test asserts < 200ms on 100k seeded rows.
  • Query plan CI: EXPLAIN assertions catch index regression before deploy.
  • Concurrency test: 50 concurrent requests; zero 503 errors or pool timeouts.
  • Contract validation: Frontend Pact expectations match real API responses in integration test.
